questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Write the balanced chemical equations for the following word equations: (i) Manganese oxide + hydrochloric acid → manganese chloride + chlorine + water (ii) Calcium hydroxide + carbon dioxide → calcium carbonate + water (iii) Zinc carbonate → zinc oxide + carbon dioxide
questions.correctAnswer: (i) MnO₂ + 4HCl → MnCl₂ + 2H₂O + Cl₂ (ii) Ca(OH)₂ + CO₂ → CaCO₃ + H₂O (iii) ZnCO₃ → ZnO + CO₂
questions.explanation: (i) MnO₂ + 4HCl → MnCl₂ + 2H₂O + Cl₂ (ii) Ca(OH)₂ + CO₂ → CaCO₃ + H₂O (iii) ZnCO₃ → ZnO + CO₂

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) Magnesium ribbon burns with a dazzling white flame in the air and changes into magnesium oxide. Mention the type of this reaction. (b) List out the observations that help us to determine that a chemical reaction has taken place.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Chemical combination reaction (synthesis reaction). (b) Indicators that a reaction has occurred: Change in colour; Evolution of a gas; Change in temperature; Change in state; Formation of precipitate. (Any two)
questions.explanation: (a) Chemical combination reaction (synthesis reaction). (b) Indicators that a reaction has occurred: Change in colour; Evolution of a gas; Change in temperature; Change in state; Formation of precipitate. (Any two)

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) Chemical equations have to be balanced. Why? (b) Mention the measures that are followed to prevent rusting of iron.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) According to the law of conservation of mass, the number of atoms of each element remains the same before and after a chemical reaction. So equations must be balanced. (b) Oiling; Greasing; Painting; Galvanisation; Chrome plating; Anodisation. (Any four)
questions.explanation: (a) According to the law of conservation of mass, the number of atoms of each element remains the same before and after a chemical reaction. So equations must be balanced. (b) Oiling; Greasing; Painting; Galvanisation; Chrome plating; Anodisation. (Any four)

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) What is acid rain? (b) Which gas is liberated when an acid reacts with a metal carbonate? (c) Write the chemical name and molecular formula of baking soda.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: A solution of baking soda is commonly applied to the area of a honey bee sting. Justify this action.
questions.correctAnswer: Sting of honey bee contains methanoic acid (formic acid) which causes pain and irritation. Applying baking soda (sodium hydrogen carbonate — a mild base) on the stung area neutralises the acid and gives relief.
questions.explanation: Sting of honey bee contains methanoic acid (formic acid) which causes pain and irritation. Applying baking soda (sodium hydrogen carbonate — a mild base) on the stung area neutralises the acid and gives relief.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Hydrochloric acid is considered as a strong acid. Why?
questions.correctAnswer: Hydrochloric acid completely dissociates (ionises) in water to produce a large number of hydrogen ions (H⁺) / hydronium ions (H₃O⁺). Since it completely ionises, it is called a strong acid.
questions.explanation: Hydrochloric acid completely dissociates (ionises) in water to produce a large number of hydrogen ions (H⁺) / hydronium ions (H₃O⁺). Since it completely ionises, it is called a strong acid.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: How do you detect a base using blue litmus paper?
questions.correctAnswer: The colour of blue litmus paper does NOT change when dipped in a basic solution. (Blue litmus remains blue in a base. It changes to red in an acid.)
questions.explanation: The colour of blue litmus paper does NOT change when dipped in a basic solution. (Blue litmus remains blue in a base. It changes to red in an acid.)

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.chapter: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables
questions.question: (a) Explain the formation of sodium chloride molecule by the transfer of electrons. (b) What are the products obtained when aluminium metal reacts with steam?
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Na has electronic configuration 2,8,1. It loses one electron to become Na⁺ (2,8). Cl has configuration 2,8,7. It gains one electron to become Cl⁻ (2,8,8). The electrostatic attraction between Na⁺ and Cl⁻ forms NaCl. (b) Al + steam → Al₂O₃ + H₂. Aluminium oxide and hydrogen gas are produced.
questions.explanation: (a) Na has electronic configuration 2,8,1. It loses one electron to become Na⁺ (2,8). Cl has configuration 2,8,7. It gains one electron to become Cl⁻ (2,8,8). The electrostatic attraction between Na⁺ and Cl⁻ forms NaCl. (b) Al + steam → Al₂O₃ + H₂. Aluminium oxide and hydrogen gas are produced.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.chapter: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables
questions.question: (a) Show the formation of magnesium chloride molecule by transfer of electrons. (b) Aluminium oxide is called an amphoteric oxide. Why?
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Mg (2,8,2) loses 2 electrons → Mg²⁺ (2,8). Each Cl (2,8,7) gains 1 electron → Cl⁻ (2,8,8). One Mg²⁺ combines with two Cl⁻ to form MgCl₂. (b) Aluminium oxide reacts with both acid and base to produce salt and water. Al₂O₃ + 6HCl → 2AlCl₃ + 3H₂O; Al₂O₃ + 2NaOH → 2NaAlO₂ + H₂O. Hence it is amphoteric.
questions.explanation: (a) Mg (2,8,2) loses 2 electrons → Mg²⁺ (2,8). Each Cl (2,8,7) gains 1 electron → Cl⁻ (2,8,8). One Mg²⁺ combines with two Cl⁻ to form MgCl₂. (b) Aluminium oxide reacts with both acid and base to produce salt and water. Al₂O₃ + 6HCl → 2AlCl₃ + 3H₂O; Al₂O₃ + 2NaOH → 2NaAlO₂ + H₂O. Hence it is amphoteric.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.chapter: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables
questions.question: Write the properties of ionic compounds with respect to solubility, melting point, physical state and electrical conductivity.
questions.correctAnswer: Solubility: Generally soluble in water. Melting point: High melting and boiling points due to strong electrostatic forces. Physical state: Solid at room temperature; crystals. Electrical conductivity: Do not conduct electricity in solid state (ions not free to move); conduct electricity in molten state or in aqueous solution.
questions.explanation: Solubility: Generally soluble in water. Melting point: High melting and boiling points due to strong electrostatic forces. Physical state: Solid at room temperature; crystals. Electrical conductivity: Do not conduct electricity in solid state (ions not free to move); conduct electricity in molten state or in aqueous solution.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.chapter: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables
questions.question: Give reasons: (i) Magnesium metal displaces copper in copper sulphate solution. (ii) Sodium metal is stored in kerosene.
questions.correctAnswer: (i) Magnesium is more reactive than copper (higher in the reactivity series). A more reactive metal displaces a less reactive metal from its salt solution. Mg + CuSO₄ → MgSO₄ + Cu (ii) Sodium is a very reactive metal. It reacts vigorously with oxygen, moisture (water) and air. Storing in kerosene prevents contact with air and moisture and thus prevents unwanted chemical reactions.
questions.explanation: (i) Magnesium is more reactive than copper (higher in the reactivity series). A more reactive metal displaces a less reactive metal from its salt solution. Mg + CuSO₄ → MgSO₄ + Cu (ii) Sodium is a very reactive metal. It reacts vigorously with oxygen, moisture (water) and air. Storing in kerosene prevents contact with air and moisture and thus prevents unwanted chemical reactions.

questions.question: What is roasting in metallurgy?
questions.correctAnswer: Roasting is the process of heating a sulphide ore strongly in the presence of excess air (oxygen) to convert it into the metal oxide. 2ZnS + 3O₂ → 2ZnO + 2SO₂

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.chapter: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables
questions.question: How is pure mercury obtained from cinnabar?
questions.correctAnswer: Cinnabar (HgS) is roasted in the presence of air to obtain mercurous oxide (HgO) and sulphur dioxide. 2HgS + 3O₂ → 2HgO + 2SO₂. Mercurous oxide is then calcinated (heated further) to decompose it into mercury and oxygen. 2HgO → 2Hg + O₂. The mercury vapour is then condensed.
questions.explanation: Cinnabar (HgS) is roasted in the presence of air to obtain mercurous oxide (HgO) and sulphur dioxide. 2HgS + 3O₂ → 2HgO + 2SO₂. Mercurous oxide is then calcinated (heated further) to decompose it into mercury and oxygen. 2HgO → 2Hg + O₂. The mercury vapour is then condensed.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Carbon compounds are found in larger numbers in nature. Why? Clarify the reasons.
questions.correctAnswer: Two unique properties make carbon form the largest number of compounds: (1) Tetravalency: Carbon has a valency of four. It is capable of bonding with four other atoms (carbon or other elements). This gives variety of structures. (2) Catenation: Carbon has a unique ability to form bonds with other carbon atoms, giving rise to large molecules. These chains can be straight, branched or ring-shaped. Due to these properties, millions of carbon compounds exist.
questions.explanation: Two unique properties make carbon form the largest number of compounds: (1) Tetravalency: Carbon has a valency of four. It is capable of bonding with four other atoms (carbon or other elements). This gives variety of structures. (2) Catenation: Carbon has a unique ability to form bonds with other carbon atoms, giving rise to large molecules. These chains can be straight, branched or ring-shaped. Due to these properties, millions of carbon compounds exist.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) How many single bonds and double bonds are there in the molecule of C₃H₇COOH? (b) Write the structural formulae of cyclopentane and propanol.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) C₃H₇COOH = butanoic acid = CH₃CH₂CH₂COOH. Carbon skeleton: 4 carbons. Single bonds: C-C bonds (3) + C-H bonds (7) + C-OH bond (1) + O-H bond (1) = 12 single bonds. Double bond: C=O bond (1) = 1 double bond. (b) Cyclopentane: pentagon ring of 5 CH₂ groups. Propanol: CH₃-CH₂-CH₂-OH (propan-1-ol structural formula).
questions.explanation: (a) C₃H₇COOH = butanoic acid = CH₃CH₂CH₂COOH. Carbon skeleton: 4 carbons. Single bonds: C-C bonds (3) + C-H bonds (7) + C-OH bond (1) + O-H bond (1) = 12 single bonds. Double bond: C=O bond (1) = 1 double bond. (b) Cyclopentane: pentagon ring of 5 CH₂ groups. Propanol: CH₃-CH₂-CH₂-OH (propan-1-ol structural formula).

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) Write any two differences between saturated and unsaturated carbon compounds. (b) Write the molecular formula and structural formula of the following: (i) Propanoic acid (ii) Cyclohexane
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Saturated: Contains only single bonds; general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ (alkanes); less reactive; do not decolourise bromine water. Unsaturated: Contains double/triple bonds; more reactive; decolourise bromine water. (b)(i) Propanoic acid: CH₃CH₂COOH (C₃H₆O₂). Structural: CH₃-CH₂-COOH. (ii) Cyclohexane: C₆H₁₂. Six-membered carbon ring, each with two H atoms.
questions.explanation: (a) Saturated: Contains only single bonds; general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ (alkanes); less reactive; do not decolourise bromine water. Unsaturated: Contains double/triple bonds; more reactive; decolourise bromine water. (b)(i) Propanoic acid: CH₃CH₂COOH (C₃H₆O₂). Structural: CH₃-CH₂-COOH. (ii) Cyclohexane: C₆H₁₂. Six-membered carbon ring, each with two H atoms.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the cleansing action of soap on dirt.
questions.correctAnswer: Soap molecules have a hydrophilic (water-loving) head and a hydrophobic (water-hating / oil-loving) tail. When soap is added to dirty water: The hydrophobic ends attach to the oil/grease particles. The hydrophilic ends face outward towards water. This forms spherical clusters called micelles, with dirt trapped inside. When rinsed with water, the micelles (with dirt) are washed away. This is how soap removes oily dirt.
questions.explanation: Soap molecules have a hydrophilic (water-loving) head and a hydrophobic (water-hating / oil-loving) tail. When soap is added to dirty water: The hydrophobic ends attach to the oil/grease particles. The hydrophilic ends face outward towards water. This forms spherical clusters called micelles, with dirt trapped inside. When rinsed with water, the micelles (with dirt) are washed away. This is how soap removes oily dirt.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Carbon atoms do not form C⁴⁺ cation ions or C⁴⁻ anions. Why?
questions.correctAnswer: If carbon loses four electrons to form C⁴⁺, it would require a very large amount of energy to remove four electrons from the nucleus. The energy required is too high — so C⁴⁺ is not formed. If carbon gains four electrons to form C⁴⁻, it would be difficult for the 6 protons in the nucleus to hold on to 10 electrons (6+4). The nucleus cannot attract these extra electrons. Hence carbon shares electrons (covalent bond) instead.
questions.explanation: If carbon loses four electrons to form C⁴⁺, it would require a very large amount of energy to remove four electrons from the nucleus. The energy required is too high — so C⁴⁺ is not formed. If carbon gains four electrons to form C⁴⁻, it would be difficult for the 6 protons in the nucleus to hold on to 10 electrons (6+4). The nucleus cannot attract these extra electrons. Hence carbon shares electrons (covalent bond) instead.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Write the structural formula of an unsaturated carbon compound containing four carbon atoms and a ketone functional group.
questions.correctAnswer: CH₃-CO-CH=CH₂ (but-3-en-2-one) OR CH₃-C(=O)-CH₂ is a partial. For 4 carbons with ketone (C=O in middle): CH₃COCH₂ has only 3 carbons. So: CH₃-CO-C≡CH (but-3-yn-2-one) or CH₂=CH-CO-CH₃ (but-3-en-2-one): CH₂=C-CO-CH₃
questions.explanation: CH₃-CO-CH=CH₂ (but-3-en-2-one) OR CH₃-C(=O)-CH₂ is a partial. For 4 carbons with ketone (C=O in middle): CH₃COCH₂ has only 3 carbons. So: CH₃-CO-C≡CH (but-3-yn-2-one) or CH₂=CH-CO-CH₃ (but-3-en-2-one): CH₂=C-CO-CH₃

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What are the applications of the thermite reaction?
questions.correctAnswer: Applications of thermite reaction: (i) To join (weld) railway tracks (thermite welding). (ii) To join broken machine parts. (The thermite reaction produces intense heat by the reduction of iron oxide by aluminium: 2Al + Fe₂O₃ → Al₂O₃ + 2Fe + Heat)
questions.explanation: Applications of thermite reaction: (i) To join (weld) railway tracks (thermite welding). (ii) To join broken machine parts. (The thermite reaction produces intense heat by the reduction of iron oxide by aluminium: 2Al + Fe₂O₃ → Al₂O₃ + 2Fe + Heat)

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Name the catalyst used and the product obtained in the hydrogenation of ethene.
questions.correctAnswer: In the hydrogenation of ethene: CH₂=CH₂ + H₂ → CH₃-CH₃ (ethane). Catalyst used: Nickel (Ni) or Palladium (Pd) or Platinum (Pt) — generally nickel. Product obtained: Ethane (saturated hydrocarbon).
questions.explanation: In the hydrogenation of ethene: CH₂=CH₂ + H₂ → CH₃-CH₃ (ethane). Catalyst used: Nickel (Ni) or Palladium (Pd) or Platinum (Pt) — generally nickel. Product obtained: Ethane (saturated hydrocarbon).

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The molecular formula of the first member of a homologous series is CH₂O (HCHO). Determine the name and molecular formula of the third member of this group. Mention the general name of this group.
questions.correctAnswer: First member: HCHO (methanal / formaldehyde). Each successive member adds CH₂. Second member: CH₃CHO (ethanal / acetaldehyde). Third member: CH₃CH₂CHO — molecular formula C₃H₆O (propanal / propionaldehyde). General name of group: Aldehydes (general formula: R-CHO).
questions.explanation: First member: HCHO (methanal / formaldehyde). Each successive member adds CH₂. Second member: CH₃CHO (ethanal / acetaldehyde). Third member: CH₃CH₂CHO — molecular formula C₃H₆O (propanal / propionaldehyde). General name of group: Aldehydes (general formula: R-CHO).

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the formation of ionic bond between sodium and chlorine atoms.
questions.correctAnswer: Sodium (Na): Electronic configuration 2,8,1. It has 1 electron in its outermost shell. Sodium loses this electron to become Na⁺ ion (2,8) — stable octet. Chlorine (Cl): Configuration 2,8,7. It needs one electron to complete its octet. Cl gains one electron to become Cl⁻ ion (2,8,8). The electrostatic attraction between the oppositely charged Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions forms the ionic bond, creating NaCl.
questions.explanation: Sodium (Na): Electronic configuration 2,8,1. It has 1 electron in its outermost shell. Sodium loses this electron to become Na⁺ ion (2,8) — stable octet. Chlorine (Cl): Configuration 2,8,7. It needs one electron to complete its octet. Cl gains one electron to become Cl⁻ ion (2,8,8). The electrostatic attraction between the oppositely charged Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions forms the ionic bond, creating NaCl.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) List the three methods of contraception that are usually followed by human beings. (b) What is the function of the vas deferens and prostate gland in the human male reproductive system?
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Methods of contraception: (i) Barrier methods — condoms (male/female), diaphragm (ii) Chemical methods — oral contraceptive pills (iii) Intrauterine devices — Copper-T. Other methods: Surgical — vasectomy (male), tubectomy (female). (b) Vas deferens: Carries sperms from the testes to the urethra. Prostate gland: Secretes a fluid that nourishes the sperms and forms part of the semen. It also activates sperms and helps in their transport.
questions.explanation: (a) Methods of contraception: (i) Barrier methods — condoms (male/female), diaphragm (ii) Chemical methods — oral contraceptive pills (iii) Intrauterine devices — Copper-T. Other methods: Surgical — vasectomy (male), tubectomy (female). (b) Vas deferens: Carries sperms from the testes to the urethra. Prostate gland: Secretes a fluid that nourishes the sperms and forms part of the semen. It also activates sperms and helps in their transport.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: The respiration rate of aquatic animals is faster than that found in terrestrial animals. Why?
questions.correctAnswer: Because the amount of dissolved oxygen in water is fairly low compared to the amount of oxygen in the air. To meet their oxygen needs, aquatic animals have to breathe (move water over their gills) much faster.
questions.explanation: Because the amount of dissolved oxygen in water is fairly low compared to the amount of oxygen in the air. To meet their oxygen needs, aquatic animals have to breathe (move water over their gills) much faster.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) What is anaerobic respiration? What products will be produced in this process? (b) What is double circulation of blood? How is this type of blood circulation advantageous to birds and mammals?
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Anaerobic respiration: Breakdown of glucose without oxygen. Products in yeast: glucose → ethanol + CO₂ + energy. Products in muscle cells: glucose → lactic acid + energy. (b) Double circulation: Blood passes through the heart twice in one complete circulation — once through the right side (pulmonary circuit — to lungs) and once through the left side (systemic circuit — to body). Advantage: Oxygenated and deoxygenated blood are kept separate. This allows efficient supply of oxygen to body cells, which generates the extra energy needed to maintain constant body temperature in warm-blooded birds and mammals.
questions.explanation: (a) Anaerobic respiration: Breakdown of glucose without oxygen. Products in yeast: glucose → ethanol + CO₂ + energy. Products in muscle cells: glucose → lactic acid + energy. (b) Double circulation: Blood passes through the heart twice in one complete circulation — once through the right side (pulmonary circuit — to lungs) and once through the left side (systemic circuit — to body). Advantage: Oxygenated and deoxygenated blood are kept separate. This allows efficient supply of oxygen to body cells, which generates the extra energy needed to maintain constant body temperature in warm-blooded birds and mammals.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is the role of the following enzymes in the human alimentary canal? (i) Trypsin (ii) Amylase (iii) Lipase
questions.correctAnswer: (i) Trypsin: Secreted by the pancreas. Digests proteins into smaller peptides/amino acids. Acts in the small intestine. (ii) Amylase: Secreted by salivary glands (salivary amylase) and pancreas (pancreatic amylase). Digests starch (carbohydrates) into maltose/sugars. Acts in the mouth and small intestine. (iii) Lipase: Secreted by the pancreas. Digests fats (lipids) into fatty acids and glycerol. Acts in the small intestine.
questions.explanation: (i) Trypsin: Secreted by the pancreas. Digests proteins into smaller peptides/amino acids. Acts in the small intestine. (ii) Amylase: Secreted by salivary glands (salivary amylase) and pancreas (pancreatic amylase). Digests starch (carbohydrates) into maltose/sugars. Acts in the mouth and small intestine. (iii) Lipase: Secreted by the pancreas. Digests fats (lipids) into fatty acids and glycerol. Acts in the small intestine.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Double circulation of the blood is essential to maintain constant temperature in mammals. How?
questions.correctAnswer: Double circulation allows more efficient supply of oxygen to the body cells. It ensures that oxygenated and deoxygenated blood are completely separated. This generates the extra energy needed to maintain constant (warm) body temperature in mammals.
questions.explanation: Double circulation allows more efficient supply of oxygen to the body cells. It ensures that oxygenated and deoxygenated blood are completely separated. This generates the extra energy needed to maintain constant (warm) body temperature in mammals.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Draw a diagram showing the schematic sectional view of the human heart. Label the following parts: (i) Septum (ii) Right atrium

The septum divides the right and left sides. The right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from the body. The left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood to the body through the aorta.

questions.question: What is reflex arc? In many animals reflex arcs have been found an effective way of functioning. Why?
questions.correctAnswer: Reflex arc: The pathway through which the impulses of reflexes travel to set a response. It goes from receptor → sensory neuron → spinal cord → motor neuron → effector (muscle/gland). Why effective: Because the thinking process of the brain is not fast enough to respond quickly to sudden stimuli. Many animals have little complex neuron network for thinking. The reflex arc allows quick responses without brain involvement, which is faster and more efficient.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is DNA replication? How does re-establishment of chromosome number occur in sexual reproduction?
questions.correctAnswer: DNA replication: During the interphase of cell division, the cellular DNA duplicates itself (makes an exact copy). This process is called DNA replication. Each daughter cell gets a complete copy of the DNA. Re-establishment of chromosome number: In sexual reproduction, gametes (eggs and sperms) are formed by meiosis — a special type of cell division that halves the chromosome number (n = haploid). When two gametes (each with n chromosomes) fuse during fertilisation, the resulting zygote has the full diploid number (2n) restored. This maintains the chromosome number constant across generations.
questions.explanation: DNA replication: During the interphase of cell division, the cellular DNA duplicates itself (makes an exact copy). This process is called DNA replication. Each daughter cell gets a complete copy of the DNA. When two gametes (each with n chromosomes) fuse during fertilisation, the resulting zygote has the full diploid number (2n) restored. This maintains the chromosome number constant across generations.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is placenta? What is its function? In human body, testes are located in scrotum outside the abdominal cavity. Why?
questions.correctAnswer: Placenta: A special tissue/disc-shaped structure that connects the developing embryo/foetus to the wall of the uterus in placental mammals. Function of placenta: (i) Provides nourishment (glucose, oxygen) from mother's blood to the foetus. (ii) Removes waste products (CO₂, urea) from foetus to mother's blood. (iii) Provides immune protection. (iv) Produces hormones during pregnancy. Testes in scrotum: Sperms are sensitive to temperature. The temperature inside the abdominal cavity is higher than the required temperature for sperm production. The scrotum is outside the abdominal cavity where the temperature is 2-3°C lower, which is optimal for sperm production and storage.
questions.explanation: Placenta: A special tissue/disc-shaped structure that connects the developing embryo/foetus to the wall of the uterus in placental mammals. Function of placenta: (i) Provides nourishment (glucose, oxygen) from mother's blood to the foetus. (ii) Removes waste products (CO₂, urea) from foetus to mother's blood. (iii) Provides immune protection. (iv) Produces hormones during pregnancy. Testes in scrotum: Sperms are sensitive to temperature. The temperature inside the abdominal cavity is higher than the required temperature for sperm production. questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) Sexual type of reproduction leads to more variations. Justify. (b) In women's uterus the role of placenta is significant for the development of foetus.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Sexual reproduction involves fusion of gametes from two different individuals. Gametes are formed by meiosis which creates new combinations of chromosomes. Random fertilisation further increases variety. Additionally, DNA copying during meiosis has small errors creating variations. These variations help species adapt to changing environments — survival advantage. (b) The placenta provides a large surface area for exchange of nutrients, oxygen, and waste products between mother's blood and foetus blood. It nourishes the foetus throughout development. Without placenta, the foetus cannot get nutrition or eliminate waste.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) Father only determines the sex of a child. Why? (b) Yellow seed producing pea plant (YY) is crossed with green seed producing plant (yy). Draw a checker board showing the results obtained in F₂ generation.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Females have XX chromosomes. All males have XY chromosomes. Females can only give X chromosomes to gametes. Father (XY) can give either X or Y chromosome to gametes. If father gives X → XX (girl); if father gives Y → XY (boy). Since father has the ability to give X or Y chromosome, father alone determines the sex of the child. (b) F₁: YY × yy → All Yy (yellow seeds). F₁ × F₁: Yy × Yy. Checker board F₂: YY, Yy, Yy, yy. Phenotypic ratio: 3 yellow : 1 green. Genotypic ratio: 1YY : 2Yy : 1yy.

(b) F₁: YY × yy → All Yy (yellow seeds). F₁ × F₁: Yy × Yy. Checker board F₂: YY, Yy, Yy, yy. Phenotypic ratio: 3 yellow : 1 green. Genotypic ratio: 1YY : 2Yy : 1yy.

Checker board produces 16 combinations in F₂: 9 T_R_ (tall red) : 3 T_rr (tall white) : 3 ttR_ (short red) : 1 ttrr (short white). Ratio = 9:3:3:1.

Checker board produces 16 combinations in F₂: 9 T_R_ (tall red) : 3 T_rr (tall white) : 3 ttR_ (short red) : 1 ttrr (short white). Ratio = 9:3:3:1.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: State the laws of refraction of light.
questions.correctAnswer: Laws of Refraction (Snell's Law): 1st Law: The incident ray, the refracted ray and the normal to the interface of two media at the point of incidence — all lie in the same plane. 2nd Law: The ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is constant for a given pair of media. sin i / sin r = n₂/n₁ = constant. This constant is called the refractive index.

(b) u = -30 cm, v = -10 cm. 1/f = 1/v − 1/u = 1/(−10) − 1/(−30) = −1/10 + 1/30 = −3/30 + 1/30 = −2/30. f = −15 cm. Negative focal length → Concave lens. Virtual image formed on same side as object (v is negative).

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) What is the colour of danger signal lights? Why is that colour preferred? (b) Why does the sky appear blue?
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Red. Red is preferred because it has the longest wavelength and is scattered the least by fog, smoke and rain — it can be seen from great distances in adverse weather. (b) The sky appears blue due to scattering of light (Rayleigh scattering). Sunlight consists of all colours. Fine particles and gas molecules in the atmosphere scatter shorter wavelengths (blue, violet) more than longer wavelengths (red). Blue light is scattered in all directions reaching our eyes from the sky. Hence the sky appears blue.
questions.explanation: (a) Red. Red is preferred because it has the longest wavelength and is scattered the least by fog, smoke and rain — it can be seen from great distances in adverse weather. (b) The sky appears blue due to scattering of light (Rayleigh scattering). Sunlight consists of all colours. Fine particles and gas molecules in the atmosphere scatter shorter wavelengths (blue, violet) more than longer wavelengths (red). Blue light is scattered in all directions reaching our eyes from the sky. Hence the sky appears blue.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Why does the sun appear reddish at the time of sunrise and sunset?
questions.correctAnswer: At sunrise and sunset, the sun is near the horizon. Sunlight has to travel a greater distance through the atmosphere to reach our eyes. The shorter wavelengths (blue, green, violet) are scattered away. Only longer wavelengths (red, orange) reach our eyes. Hence the sun appears reddish at sunrise and sunset.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What are the reasons for overload in an electric circuit?
questions.correctAnswer: Reasons for overload: (i) Direct contact between live wire and neutral wire (short circuit). (ii) Accidental hike in the supplied voltage. (iii) Connecting too many appliances to a single socket. (iv) Faulty appliances or faulty connections.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Observe the figure showing a solenoid with switch. When switch is closed, does the B end of the solenoid attract the N end of the bar magnet? Clarify the answer.
questions.correctAnswer: No — the B end of the solenoid repels the N end of the bar magnet. When the switch is closed, the B end of the solenoid behaves like the N pole (by right-hand rule or given current direction). Since both N poles face each other, like poles repel.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Two freely suspended parallel copper wires carry current. In which situation (same direction or opposite direction) do the wires attract each other? Justify.
questions.correctAnswer: Situation 1 — when currents flow in the same direction, the wires attract each other. When current flows in the same direction in both conductors, each conductor experiences a magnetic force towards the other conductor (attraction). The direction of force depends on the direction of current — same direction currents attract, opposite direction currents repel.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What happens if the fuse is connected to the neutral wire instead of connecting to the live wire in the meter board of the house?
questions.correctAnswer: The appliances will remain live (at mains potential) even when the fuse blows. This can result in a fatal electric shock if someone touches the appliance. The electric equipment will be damaged as the fuse will not work to protect the circuit. The fuse should always be connected to the live wire for safety.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: How does the resistance of a conductor relate to (a) its length and (b) its area of cross-section?
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Resistance is directly proportional to the length of the conductor (R ∝ l). If the length doubles, resistance doubles. A longer wire offers more resistance because electrons have to travel farther through more collisions. (b) Resistance is inversely proportional to the area of cross-section (R ∝ 1/A). A thicker wire (larger area) has less resistance because more electrons can flow through at once. Formula: R = ρl/A

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) State two laws of reflection of light. (b) What is the colour of danger signal lights? Why is that colour preferred?

(b) Red colour. Preferred because red has the longest wavelength and is scattered least by particles in the atmosphere (fog, smoke, rain). Red light can travel the longest distance and remain visible.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) What are biodegradable substances? (b) Which synthetic chemical is responsible for the depletion of the ozone layer? (c) Write two examples of decomposers.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Biodegradable substances: Substances that can be broken down by biological processes (microorganisms) into simpler substances. Examples: food waste, paper, cow dung, dead plants and animals. (b) Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) — used in refrigerants and aerosols — are responsible for ozone layer depletion. (c) Decomposers: Bacteria and fungi. (e.g., Rhizobium, Penicillium, Aspergillus)

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) Grass → Frog → Snake; If the amount of energy in the first trophic level is about 100J, what is the amount of energy available for the organism at the third trophic level? (b) What are biodegradable substances? Give two examples.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) 10% law: Only 10% of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next. 1st level (Grass) = 100J. 2nd level (Frog) = 10% of 100 = 10J. 3rd level (Snake) = 10% of 10 = 1J. Energy available for snake = 1J. (b) Biodegradable substances: Materials that can be broken down by microorganisms. Examples: dead leaves, vegetable peels, paper, cotton, wool.
questions.explanation: (a) 10% law: Only 10% of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next. 1st level (Grass) = 100J. 2nd level (Frog) = 10% of 100 = 10J. 3rd level (Snake) = 10% of 10 = 1J. Energy available for snake = 1J. (b) Biodegradable substances: Materials that can be broken down by microorganisms. Examples: dead leaves, vegetable peels, paper, cotton, wool.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) What is biological magnification? (b) Write the full form of DDT. Why was the use of DDT discouraged?
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Biological magnification (biomagnification): The progressive increase in concentration of a harmful/toxic substance (like pesticides) in the bodies of organisms at successive higher trophic levels in a food chain. Organisms at higher trophic levels accumulate more toxins. (b) DDT: Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane. Use discouraged because: DDT is non-biodegradable; it accumulates in the food chain (biomagnification); it harms non-target organisms including birds and mammals; it persists in the environment for long periods causing ecological damage.
questions.explanation: (a) Biological magnification (biomagnification): The progressive increase in concentration of a harmful/toxic substance (like pesticides) in the bodies of organisms at successive higher trophic levels in a food chain. questions.question: What is a redox reaction? Give one example.
questions.correctAnswer: A redox reaction involves simultaneous oxidation and reduction. Oxidation = loss of electrons. Reduction = gain of electrons. Example: CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O. CuO is reduced (loses O). H₂ is oxidised (gains O). Both occur simultaneously.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is pH? Why is it important in daily life?
questions.correctAnswer: pH measures hydrogen ion concentration. Scale 0-14. pH<7 = acidic, pH=7 = neutral, pH>7 = basic. Importance: Stomach acid pH 1.5-3.5. Tooth decay starts at pH<5.5. Acid rain pH<5.6. Blood pH must remain 7.35-7.45. Soil pH affects crop growth.

questions.question: What is the reactivity series of metals? What is its importance?
questions.correctAnswer: Reactivity series: K>Na>Ca>Mg>Al>Zn>Fe>Sn>Pb>H>Cu>Hg>Ag>Au>Pt. Importance: Predicts reactions with acids/water. More reactive metal displaces less reactive from salt solution. Helps in extraction. Determines uses of metals.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is a homologous series? Give an example.
questions.correctAnswer: A series with same general formula, similar chemical properties, differing by CH₂ group. Example — Alkanes: CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈, C₄H₁₀. General formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. Properties vary gradually.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is transpiration? What are its advantages to plants?
questions.correctAnswer: Transpiration: Loss of water as vapour through leaves (mainly through stomata). Advantages: Creates transpirational pull — helps water move upward from roots. Cools the plant. Helps absorption of mineral salts. Maintains water balance. Contributes to water cycle.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What are plant hormones? Name four plant hormones and their functions.
questions.correctAnswer: Plant hormones regulate growth and development. 1. Auxin — cell elongation; phototropism. 2. Gibberellin — stem elongation, seed germination, flowering. 3. Cytokinin — cell division, delays leaf aging. 4. Abscisic acid (ABA) — inhibits growth, causes dormancy, closes stomata in drought.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What are the advantages of sexual reproduction over asexual reproduction?
questions.correctAnswer: Sexual reproduction: Creates genetic diversity from two parents. Variations help species adapt to changing environments. Natural selection improves species. Harmful mutations can be eliminated. Allows evolution of new species. Asexual reproduction produces identical clones with less variation and adaptability.
questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction: Creates genetic diversity from two parents. Variations help species adapt to changing environments. Natural selection improves species. questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is the difference between dominant and recessive traits? Give one example.
questions.correctAnswer: Dominant: Appears in F₁ when pure-breeding organisms with different traits cross. Denoted by capital letter (T=tall). Recessive: Suppressed in F₁, reappears in F₂ in 25% offspring. Denoted by small letter (t=dwarf). Example: Tall (TT)×Dwarf (tt): F₁ all Tt (tall dominant). F₂: 3 tall:1 dwarf.
questions.explanation: Dominant: Appears in F₁ when pure-breeding organisms with different traits cross. Denoted by capital letter (T=tall). Recessive: Suppressed in F₁, reappears in F₂ in 25% offspring. Denoted by small letter (t=dwarf). Example: Tall (TT)×Dwarf (tt): F₁ all Tt (tall dominant). F₂: 3 tall:1 dwarf.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is myopia? How can it be corrected?
questions.correctAnswer: Myopia (short-sightedness): Cannot see distant objects clearly. Cause: Eyeball too long OR lens too convex — image forms in front of retina. Correction: Concave (diverging) lens of appropriate focal length. Concave lens diverges rays so image falls on retina.
questions.explanation: Myopia (short-sightedness): Cannot see distant objects clearly. Cause: Eyeball too long OR lens too convex — image forms in front of retina. Correction: Concave (diverging) lens of appropriate focal length. Concave lens diverges rays so image falls on retina.

questions.question: A wire of resistance 20Ω is bent into a circle. What is the effective resistance between two points at the ends of a diameter?
questions.correctAnswer: Each half = 10Ω. Two halves in parallel. 1/R = 1/10 + 1/10 = 2/10. R = 5Ω.
questions.explanation: Each half = 10Ω. Two halves in parallel. 1/R = 1/10 + 1/10 = 2/10. R = 5Ω.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: State Fleming's Left-Hand Rule. Mention its application.
questions.correctAnswer: Stretch thumb, forefinger and middle finger of left hand mutually perpendicular. Forefinger = magnetic field direction. Middle finger = current direction. Thumb = direction of force/motion of conductor. Application: Determines force on current-carrying conductor in magnetic field — used in electric motors.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is an ecosystem? Give two examples.
questions.correctAnswer: Ecosystem: Community of living organisms (biotic — plants, animals, microorganisms) interacting with each other and non-living (abiotic — air, water, soil, temperature, sunlight) components. Examples: 1. Forest ecosystem. 2. Pond ecosystem. Maintains energy flow through food chains and nutrient cycling.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is the difference between aerobic and anaerobic respiration?
questions.correctAnswer: Aerobic: With oxygen. C₆H₁₂O₆+6O₂→6CO₂+6H₂O+38ATP. More energy. In mitochondria. Anaerobic: Without oxygen. Yeast: glucose→ethanol+CO₂+2ATP. Muscles: glucose→lactic acid+2ATP. Less energy. In cytoplasm.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What are olfactory indicators? How do acids and bases affect them? Give two examples.

Vanilla essence: Retained in acid, disappears in base. How they work: Active compounds react with bases, altering molecular structure and changing smell. Useful for people with colour blindness.

questions.question: What is electrolytic refining? Explain with description for copper refining.
questions.correctAnswer: Electrolytic refining: Obtaining highly pure metals from impure metals using electrolysis. Copper refining: Anode = impure copper (blister copper). Cathode = pure copper strip. Electrolyte = acidified CuSO₄ solution. Process: Anode dissolves (Cu→Cu²⁺+2e⁻). Cu²⁺ ions migrate to cathode. Cathode deposits pure copper (Cu²⁺+2e⁻→Cu). Impurities settle as anode mud (gold, silver). 99.9% pure copper obtained.
questions.explanation: Electrolytic refining: Obtaining highly pure metals from impure metals using electrolysis. Copper refining: Anode = impure copper (blister copper). Cathode = pure copper strip. questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Write any three differences between soaps and detergents.

Detergents work in hard water. 3. Biodegradability: Soaps are biodegradable. Some detergents are non-biodegradable, causing water pollution. 4. Raw material: Soaps from natural fats/oils. Detergents from petroleum. 5. pH: Soaps mildly alkaline; some detergents neutral.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the process of nutrition in Amoeba (holozoic nutrition).
questions.correctAnswer: Holozoic nutrition steps: 1. Ingestion: Amoeba engulfs food by extending pseudopodia. Food enclosed in food vacuole. 2. Digestion: Digestive enzymes secreted into food vacuole — intracellular digestion. 3. Absorption: Digested food absorbed into cytoplasm. 4. Assimilation: Used for energy and body building. 5. Egestion: Undigested waste expelled through cell membrane. No fixed opening for ingestion or egestion.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the structure and function of the neuron (nerve cell).
questions.correctAnswer: Neuron: Structural and functional unit of nervous system. Structure: Cell body (cyton) — nucleus and cytoplasm. Dendrites — short branched projections that receive impulses. Axon — long single projection carrying impulse away from cell body. Myelin sheath — protective covering. Nodes of Ranvier — gaps in myelin. Axon terminal (synaptic knob) — connects to next neuron. Function: Dendrites receive → cell body → axon → axon terminal. Synapse: Junction using neurotransmitters. Sensory neurons (receptor→brain), Motor neurons (brain→effector), Interneurons (connecting).
questions.explanation: Neuron: Structural and functional unit of nervous system. Structure: Cell body (cyton) — nucleus and cytoplasm. Dendrites — short branched projections that receive impulses. Axon — long single projection carrying impulse away from cell body. Myelin sheath — protective covering. Nodes of Ranvier — gaps in myelin. Axon terminal (synaptic knob) — connects to next neuron. Function: Dendrites receive → cell body → axon → axon terminal. Synapse: Junction using neurotransmitters. Sensory neurons (receptor→brain), Motor neurons (brain→effector), Interneurons (connecting).

Budding: Yeast cells — bud grows and separates. 3. Fragmentation: Spirogyra breaks into fragments. 4. Spore formation: Rhizopus, ferns, mosses. 5. questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain Mendel's Law of Segregation with an example.
questions.correctAnswer: Law of Segregation: When a pair of alleles are brought together in a hybrid, they do not mix but remain separate. During gamete formation, alleles segregate so each gamete receives only one allele. Example: Tall (TT)×Dwarf (tt) pea plant. F₁: All Tt (tall dominant). Gametes of F₁: T and t (not mixed). F₂ Tt×Tt: TT, Tt, Tt, tt → 3 tall:1 dwarf. Dwarf trait (t) present but hidden in F₁ — segregated and reappeared in F₂. Proves alleles remain pure and do not blend.
questions.explanation: Law of Segregation: When a pair of alleles are brought together in a hybrid, they do not mix but remain separate. During gamete formation, alleles segregate so each gamete receives only one allele. Example: Tall (TT)×Dwarf (tt) pea plant. F₁: All Tt (tall dominant). Gametes of F₁: T and t (not mixed). F₂ Tt×Tt: TT, Tt, Tt, tt → 3 tall:1 dwarf. Dwarf trait (t) present but hidden in F₁ — segregated and reappeared in F₂. Proves alleles remain pure and do not blend.

questions.explanation: (a) Object at C: Image formed at C itself. Real, inverted, same size (m=−1). (b) Object between F and C: Image formed beyond C. Real, inverted, enlarged (m<−1, magnified). Use: Between F and C used in projectors, searchlights.

questions.question: What is hypermetropia? Explain its cause and correction.
questions.correctAnswer: Hypermetropia (Long-sightedness): Cannot see nearby objects clearly. Cause: Eyeball too short OR lens too thin/flat — image of nearby objects forms behind retina. Correction: Convex (converging) lens. Convex lens converges diverging rays from nearby objects, making image fall on retina.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: What is electromagnetic induction? State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.
questions.correctAnswer: Electromagnetic induction: Production of EMF in a conductor when placed in a changing magnetic field or when there is relative motion between conductor and magnet. Faraday's Laws: 1st Law: When magnetic flux changes, EMF is induced. 2nd Law: Induced EMF ∝ rate of change of magnetic flux. E = −NΔΦ/Δt. Application: Used in electric generators to convert mechanical energy to electrical energy.

questions.question: What is ozone layer? Why is it important? What causes its depletion?
questions.correctAnswer: Ozone layer: Layer of O₃ molecules in stratosphere (15-35 km above Earth). Importance: Absorbs harmful UV radiation. Protects from skin cancer, cataracts, immune suppression. questions.question: (a) What is corrosion? Explain the conditions for rusting of iron. (b) Name four methods to prevent corrosion.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Corrosion: Gradual destruction of metals by chemical reaction with environment. Rusting conditions (experiment): Test tube 1 — nail in water+air → rusts. (b) Prevention: Painting. Oiling and greasing. Galvanisation (zinc coating). Electroplating. Anodisation. Chrome plating. Alloying (stainless steel). Tinning.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the structure and function of the human excretory system.
questions.correctAnswer: Human Excretory System removes nitrogenous waste (urea). Organs: Kidneys (pair): Main excretory organs. Each has ~1 million nephrons. Nephron structure: Bowman's capsule surrounds glomerulus (capillary tuft). Glomerular filtration: Blood filtered under pressure → filtrate enters Bowman's capsule. Renal tubule: Selective reabsorption of glucose, amino acids, water, Na⁺. Collecting duct: Final concentration of urine. Functions: Filtration of blood. Osmoregulation — controls water balance. Maintains blood pressure. Removes excess salts. Ureters: Carry urine from kidneys to urinary bladder. Urinary bladder: Stores urine. Urethra: Expels urine.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Describe the menstrual cycle and explain what happens if egg is fertilized or not fertilized.
questions.correctAnswer: Menstrual cycle (~28 days): Day 1-5: Menstruation — uterine lining sheds. Day 6-13: Uterine lining rebuilds. Day 14: Ovulation — egg released from ovary. Day 15-28: Uterine lining thickens further. If NOT fertilized: Corpus luteum degenerates. Progesterone falls. Uterine lining breaks down — menstruation begins. If IS fertilized: Sperm fertilizes egg in fallopian tube. Zygote formed. Zygote divides → embryo → implants in uterine wall. HCG maintains corpus luteum. Progesterone maintains pregnancy. Menstruation does not occur. Pregnancy lasts 9 months.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the various sources of variation in organisms. Why are variations important?
questions.correctAnswer: Sources: 1. Errors in DNA copying: Minor mutations during replication. 2. Meiosis: Independent assortment of chromosomes. Crossing over. 3. Random fertilization: Any sperm + any egg — enormous diversity. 4. Environmental factors: Somatic modifications. 5. Mutations: Due to radiation, chemicals. Importance: Natural selection — variations help survival in changing environments. Evolution — accumulated variations lead to new species. Adaptability — organisms with beneficial variations reproduce more. Genetic diversity protects species from extinction. Basis of selective breeding in agriculture.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the structure and functions of the human eye. What is the power of accommodation?
questions.correctAnswer: Structure: Cornea (refracts most light), Iris (controls pupil), Pupil (controls light), Lens (fine focuses on retina), Retina (rods and cones), Optic nerve (signals to brain), Aqueous humour, Vitreous humour. Function: Light → cornea → pupil → lens focuses → retina → optic nerve → brain. Power of Accommodation: Ability of eye lens to change focal length to focus on objects at different distances. Near: ciliary muscles tighten → lens thicker → shorter focal length. Far: ciliary muscles relax → lens thinner → longer focal length. Normal eye range: near point (25 cm) to far point (infinity).

questions.question: (a) Derive the formula for equivalent resistance when resistors are in series. (b) Three resistors 3Ω, 4Ω, 5Ω connected to 24V battery in series. Find equivalent resistance, current, and voltage across each.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Series: Same current I. V=V₁+V₂+V₃=IR₁+IR₂+IR₃=I(R₁+R₂+R₃). Rs=R₁+R₂+R₃. (b) Rs=3+4+5=12Ω. I=24/12=2A. V₁=2×3=6V. V₂=2×4=8V. V₃=2×5=10V. Check: 6+8+10=24V ✓.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the working principle of an electric generator. What is the difference between AC and DC generator?
questions.correctAnswer: Electric Generator: Converts mechanical energy to electrical energy using electromagnetic induction. Principle (Faraday's law): When coil rotates in magnetic field, flux changes → EMF induced → current flows. Working: Rectangular coil rotates between magnet poles. Changing flux induces EMF. AC Generator: Slip rings. Current alternates direction each half rotation. Used in power stations. DC Generator: Split-ring commutator. Output current always in same direction. Used in motors, electroplating. Difference: AC uses slip rings, current alternates; DC uses commutator, current unidirectional. Indian supply: 220V, 50Hz AC.

Used in motors, electroplating. Difference: AC uses slip rings, current alternates; DC uses commutator, current unidirectional. Indian supply: 220V, 50Hz AC.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) Explain the different types of food chains. (b) What is the 10% law of energy transfer? Explain with an example.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Food chains: 1. Grazing food chain: Starts with producers. Grass→Grasshopper→Frog→Snake→Eagle. Most common. 2. Detritus food chain: Starts with dead organic matter. Dead leaves→Earthworm→Robin→Hawk. Important in forest/soil. (b) 10% Law: Only 10% of energy at one trophic level transfers to next. 90% lost as heat and metabolic activities. Example: Grass (1000J)→Grasshopper (100J)→Frog (10J)→Snake (1J)→Eagle (0.1J). Food chains limited to 3-5 links. Explains why vegetarian diet is more energy-efficient.

questions.question: Explain the properties of metals and nonmetals with respect to physical and chemical properties. Give examples.
questions.correctAnswer: Physical Properties: Metals: Lustrous, good conductors of heat/electricity, hard (except Na,K), high melting/boiling points (except Hg), malleable and ductile, solid at room temperature (except Hg), sonorous. Nonmetals: Dull (except graphite), poor conductors (except graphite), soft (except diamond), low melting/boiling points, brittle, gas/liquid at room temperature (except carbon, sulphur). Chemical Properties: Metals: React with O₂ → basic metal oxides. React with water → hydroxide+H₂. React with acids → salt+H₂. Reducing agents. Form ionic compounds. Nonmetals: React with O₂ → acidic oxides. Don't react with water/acids generally. Oxidizing agents. Form covalent compounds. Examples: Metals: Fe, Cu, Al, Au, Na. Nonmetals: O₂, N₂, S, C, Cl₂, H₂.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) What are the allotropes of carbon? Explain structure and properties of two allotropes. (b) Explain the cleansing action of soap.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Diamond: Each C bonded to 4 others — tetrahedral 3D network. Very hard (hardest natural substance). No electrical conductivity. High melting point. Used in cutting tools, jewellery. Graphite: Each C bonded to 3 others in hexagonal layers. Layers held by weak forces — slippery. Conducts electricity (one free electron per C). Used as lubricant, pencils, electrodes. Fullerene (C₆₀): Spherical cage. Used in nanotechnology. (b) Cleansing action of soap: Soap molecules have hydrophilic head (water-loving) and hydrophobic tail (oil-loving). In water with oily dirt: Tails point into oil droplet. Heads point outward into water. Forms spherical micelle cluster. Dirt/oil trapped inside. Rinsed away with micelles. This is how soap removes grease.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the process of photosynthesis. What are the factors affecting photosynthesis?
questions.correctAnswer: Photosynthesis: Green plants synthesize glucose using sunlight, CO₂ and water. Equation: 6CO₂+6H₂O+Light energy→C₆H₁₂O₆+6O₂. Location: Chloroplasts (chlorophyll pigment). Two stages: 1. Light reaction (thylakoids): Light absorbed by chlorophyll. Water photolysis: 2H₂O→4H⁺+O₂+4e⁻. ATP and NADPH formed. O₂ released. 2. Dark reaction/Calvin cycle (stroma): CO₂ fixed using ATP and NADPH. Glucose synthesized. Stomata: CO₂ enters, O₂ exits, water exits. Factors: 1. Light intensity (increases rate to saturation). 2. CO₂ concentration (higher = faster). 3. Temperature (optimum 25-30°C). 4. Water (essential for photolysis). 5. Chlorophyll content. 6. Mineral salts (N,Mg,Fe needed). Importance: Produces O₂ for all aerobic life. Primary food source. Reduces CO₂ — controls climate.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: Explain the process of reproduction in flowering plants (from pollination to seed formation).
questions.correctAnswer: Flower parts: Stamen (male: anther+filament); Pistil (female: stigma+style+ovary+ovules). Pollination: Transfer of pollen from anther to stigma. Self-pollination (within plant). Cross-pollination (between plants — wind, insects, water, birds). Fertilization: Pollen germinates on stigma → pollen tube grows down style → carries two male gametes to ovule. Double fertilization: 1. Male gamete + egg → Zygote (2n). 2. Second male gamete + two polar nuclei → Endosperm (3n) — food for developing seed. Seed and Fruit formation: Zygote → embryo. Ovule → seed. Ovary wall → fruit. Seeds dispersed by wind, water, animals, explosive mechanisms. Germination: Moisture, warmth, oxygen → new plant. Significance: Creates variation through genetic combinations.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) Derive the relationship P=V²/R=I²R for electric power. (b) Three appliances — 100W bulb, 500W iron, 1000W heater — connected in parallel to 220V supply. Find current drawn by each and total current. (c) Why are household appliances connected in parallel?
questions.correctAnswer: (a) P=W/t. V=W/Q→W=VQ. P=VI. By Ohm's law V=IR: P=I²R. Also I=V/R: P=V²/R. Hence P=VI=I²R=V²/R. (b) Bulb: I=P/V=100/220=0.45A. Iron: I=500/220=2.27A. Heater: I=1000/220=4.55A. Total=7.27A. (c) Parallel advantages: Each appliance gets full 220V. Each can be switched independently. Failure of one doesn't affect others. Different power ratings work together. Standard household wiring design.

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.question: (a) What is a solenoid? Explain the magnetic field produced. (b) Explain the working of a DC motor with labelled diagram description.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Solenoid: Coil of insulated wire wound in cylinder form. Magnetic field: Each turn acts as small electromagnet. Inside: Strong, uniform field (parallel lines). Outside: Weak field like bar magnet. One end = N pole (by right-hand rule), other = S pole. Stronger than bar magnet — can be switched off, field strength varied. (b) DC Motor: Converts electrical energy to mechanical energy. Principle: Current-carrying conductor in magnetic field experiences force (Fleming's Left-Hand Rule). Components: Rectangular coil (armature) in magnetic field. Split-ring commutator — reverses current every half rotation. Carbon brushes maintain contact. Battery/DC supply. Working: Current flows → magnetic force pushes one side up, other down → coil rotates. After half rotation, commutator reverses current → coil continues same direction. Uses: Electric fans, mixers, toys, electric vehicles, washing machines.

questions.question: Explain the endocrine system in humans. Name the major endocrine glands, hormones they secrete and their functions.
questions.correctAnswer: Endocrine system: Ductless glands secreting hormones into bloodstream. Hormones are chemical messengers. Major glands: 1. Pituitary (Master gland): GH (body growth), TSH (controls thyroid), FSH/LH (reproductive), ADH (water regulation), Oxytocin (childbirth). 2. Thyroid (neck): Thyroxine — regulates metabolism, growth. Deficiency: Goitre. 3. Parathyroid: PTH — controls calcium. 4. Adrenal glands: Cortex: Cortisol (stress, metabolism). Medulla: Adrenaline (fight or flight — increases heart rate, blood pressure). 5. Pancreas: Insulin — lowers blood glucose. Glucagon — raises blood glucose. Diabetes: insulin deficiency. 6. Gonads: Testes → Testosterone (male). Ovaries → Estrogen, Progesterone (female). 7. Thymus: Thymosin — immune system. Hormones vs Nervous system: Hormones slow, long-lasting via blood. Nervous system fast, short-lived electrical impulses.
